items.q: What is the difference between overt and covert prestige?
items.correct: Overt prestige is the high status of standard varieties recognised by society (RP, Standard English); covert prestige is the hidden status of non-standard varieties within particular groups, signalling solidarity or toughness.
items.misconception: Only standard language has prestige; non-standard varieties have none.
items.random_wrong: Overt prestige is loud speech and covert prestige is quiet speech.
items.why: Covert prestige explains why people maintain non-standard varieties despite social stigma — they carry value within their communities. Students assume prestige is single-dimensional, missing why language change isn't all toward the standard.

items.q: What does Labov's research show about language variation?
items.correct: Language varies systematically by social class, gender, age and context — and speakers shift style depending on formality, often unconsciously.
items.misconception: Labov showed that working-class people speak ungrammatically.
items.random_wrong: Labov showed that everyone speaks the same way regardless of background.
items.why: Labov's finding is that variation is rule-governed, not deficient. Working-class varieties follow consistent grammars — they aren't broken Standard English. Students misread variation as error.

items.correct: Alternating between languages or varieties within a conversation or even a single utterance — often patterned by context, topic, identity or audience. Code-switching is rule-governed, not random.
items.misconception: Code-switching shows the speaker doesn't know either language properly.
items.random_wrong: Code-switching means using slang words in formal speech.
items.why: Code-switching requires fluency in both codes and follows linguistic constraints (e.g. avoiding ungrammatical splits). Students treat it as evidence of deficiency — research shows it is a sophisticated bilingual skill.

items.q: Labov's 1966 department store study targeted which variable?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Post-vocalic /r/. “Glottal stop” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Covert prestige is best associated with:
items.why: Correct answer: Peter Trudgill's Norwich study. “Robin Lakoff's deficit model” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Labov's department store study was published in:
items.why: The real figure is 1966. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
